citation.title: Electric field-driven intensification of palladium membrane separators and reactors: A multi-scale computational study

content: A B S T R A C T Palladium based membranes hold significant promise for the efficient and compact production of high-purity hydrogen. However, their performance is often hindered by the co-adsorption of gas-phase species, particularly carbon monoxide (CO), which competes with hydrogen and limits flux through these costly membranes. In this study, we employ a multi-scale modeling framework to investigate the influence of an applied electric field (AEF) on Pd-based membrane separators and reactors. At the molecular scale, density functional theory (DFT) calculations are used to estimate the adsorption energies of CO and hydrogen on Pd surfaces under varying electric fields. These results are then integrated into unit-scale simulations of membrane systems. The DFT results show that AEFs significantly alter the adsorption energy of CO — due to its polar nature — while having negligible effect on nonpolar hydrogen. Specifically, a negative electric field of -1 V/Å reduces CO adsorption energy by 0.26 eV, whereas a positive field increases it, thereby negatively impacting hydrogen flux. In a simple membrane separator, applying a -1 V/Å field reduces CO surface coverage by up to 64 % and enhances transmembrane hydrogen flux by as much as 2.5 times. For membrane reactors, we evaluate the dual impact of AEFs on both competitive adsorption and equilibrium constant.

citation.title: Electric field-driven intensification of palladium membrane separators and reactors: A multi-scale computational study

content: ogen flux by as much as 2.5 times. For membrane reactors, we evaluate the dual impact of AEFs on both competitive adsorption and equilibrium constant. Our simulations reveal that the spatial implementation of the electric field plays a critical role: when the AEF is applied only near the membrane surface, methane steam reforming conversion at 550 ◦C improves significantly—from 35 % to 70 %. However, if the AEF influences the entire reactor volume, it can reduce the equilibrium constant and thus lower overall conversion. These findings highlight the importance of reactor designs that localize electric fields at the membrane surface to maximize performance benefits.

1. Introduction Hydrogen has gained great attention as an emissions-free energy carrier, with global demand reaching 94 million tons in 2021 [1]. Its demand is expected to increase by 8-fold until 2050 [2]. There are various methods to produce hydrogen from different sources, the main ones are hydrocarbon reforming, water electrolysis and thermal conversion of biomass [3]. Among these, steam reforming, primarily utilizing methane, stands out as the predominant method for hydrogen production. In the United States, more than 95% of the hydrogen is produced by steam–methane reforming [4]. The process consists of two consecutive reactions, the steam–methane reforming reaction (SMR) Eq. (1) and the water–gas shift reaction (WGS) Eq. (2): CH4 + H2O ↔3H2 + CO(1) CO + H2O ↔H2 + CO2(2)

Steam reforming (of any hydrocarbon or alcohol) produces a hydrogen stream containing a high concentration of other components, such as CO and CO2. Therefore, several separation processes require processes to purify the hydrogen. PSA is the leading hydrogen purification technology in centralized production with a share of 85 % of global hydrogen purification [5,6]. Certain applications, like fuel cells (FC), require a feed of high-purity hydrogen, deeming efficient separation steps [7]. Membrane separation has various advantages over the conventional separation technology, in terms of low energy consumption, simplicity, high selectivity, and ease of scalability, which make membrane separation fit for small-scale distributed hydrogen production [8,9]. Potential materials for such membranes include polymeric, carbon, silica, MOFs, and dense metals [10]. Polymeric membranes are considered a low-cost solution, but due to the disorderly nature of polymeric materials, they suffer from low selectivity [11]. Carbon-based

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) membranes can be a better alternative due to higher selectivity of the molecular sieve, but it is challenging to fabricate large, defect-free membranes [6,13]. Silica membranes can efficiently separate hydrogen, but hydrothermal instability is a significant issue [9]. Conversely, dense metal membranes, especially those made from Pd alloys, demonstrate exceptional selectivity, high diffusivity, and remarkable thermal stability. Their unique hydrogen separation process achieves the highest selectivity and permeation compared to other membranes through the solution–diffusion mechanism [14,15]. The hydrogen transport is initiated by dissociative adsorption of hydrogen molecule on the membrane’s surface, resulting in the formation of atomic hydrogen. This atomic hydrogen then diffuses through the metal lattice, driven by chemical potential gradient. On the other membrane side, atomic hydrogen is recombined into molecular hydrogen H2 and subsequently diffuses away from the metallic surface [16–18]. This mechanism renders metallic membranes highly selective for hydrogen, as no other gas can effectively dissociate and diffuse through the metal.

Palladium-based membrane reactors (MRs) offer a promising solution for achieving high-purity hydrogen with low defectiveness and intensifying the process for small-scale production [19]. There are two main advantages of using MRs: MRs enable the removal of hydrogen from the reaction zone, which enhances the reaction conversion by shifting the equilibrium towards the product side at moderate temperatures. Furthermore, MRs produce high-purity hydrogen as the permeate in a single unit, eliminating the need for downstream conversion or separations steps [14,20–24]. The reaction side in palladium-based MRs often contains mixtures of methane, hydrogen, water, CO, CO2, and various other species, depending on the feed. Numerous studies have documented a substantial reduction in the hydrogen permeate flux upon the introduction of carbon monoxide into the feed stream. This decrease is attributed to the blocking of available sites on the Pd surface caused by the strong adsorption of CO, which hinders both hydrogen adsorption and dissociation [25–29]. Israni et al. noted that even in small concentrations, the presence of CO resulted in a notable decrease in hydrogen transmembrane flux, ranging from 30 % to 60 % [30].

Applied electric fields (AEF) has been suggested as a process intensification method to improve activity and selectivity of reactions [31]. An AEF above 0.1 V/Å has been found to influence the adsorption orientation and energy of molecules, reaction energies, and activation energy barriers for reactions, primarily due to a change in the distribution of electron density, polarization, or charge transfer [32]. Electric field can also significantly alter chemical equilibrium through two primary mechanisms: molecular polarization (induced dipoles) and orientation of permanent dipoles, which change the thermodynamic state of the system [33]. Testing the effect of an electric field on heterogeneous catalysts is challenging, mainly due to the challenge of achieving a consistently high electric field. In addition, Field strength can only be estimated rather than measured [32]. Experimental studies have revealed the impact of electric fields on catalysts through various reactor designs. Scanning tunneling microscope (STM) nanoreactors can operate with a high and consistent electric field, making them closely aligned with simulation results. In an STM reactor, the electric field is applied through a sharp probe, utilizing a low voltage (≈ 1 V) [34,35].

content: sely aligned with simulation results. In an STM reactor, the electric field is applied through a sharp probe, utilizing a low voltage (≈ 1 V) [34,35]. However, this system operates in vacuum conditions and is irrelevant for large-scale reactions. Several configurations have been suggested for larger-scale reactors. The first is the probe–bed–probe (PBP) reactor, which features a traditional packed catalyst bed situated between two metallic probes, one on each side of the bed [36–38]. The PBP reactors operate at high voltages, ranging from hundreds to thousands of volts, due to the greater distance between the electrodes. When a high enough current is applied, this can lead to the creation of plasma [32]. Continuous-circuit reactors are a promising approach for establishing a uniform surface electric field. In this configuration, a conductive layer, such as catalysts or a metallic membrane, is integrated into an electrical circuit. Previous experimental work that applied electric fields by incorporating a conductive catalyst used only 200 V, resulting in surface electric fields estimated to be in the range of 0.01 to 0.1 V/Å [32,39]. Theoretical investigations using density functional theory (DFT) have explored the effects of AEFs on the adsorption of variousmolecules on transition metal (TM) surfaces (e.g., Pt, Ni, Pd) [32,39–43].

content: theory (DFT) have explored the effects of AEFs on the adsorption of variousmolecules on transition metal (TM) surfaces (e.g., Pt, Ni, Pd) [32,39–43]. In the absence of an electric field, an H2O molecule prefers to adsorb nearly parallel to the on-top site of Ni(111) surface, however an electric field ranging from -1 V/Å to 1 V/Å can reorient it to an H-down structure under negative fields and an H-up structure under positive fields on both flat and stepped Ni surfaces [42]. An external electric field also significantly affects the stability of H2O on Ni surfaces, with a negative field destabilizing and a positive field enhancing adsorption, which can be explained by frontier molecular orbital theory [42]. A similar effect was observed for the adsorption of CH2OH and CH3OH on Ni(111), where the orientation of OH group shifted from an H-down to an H-up structure and adsorption energy increased (i.e. from -0.05 eV to -0.47 eV for CH3OH), as the electric field was increased from -1 V/Å to 1 V/Å [44]. Nonetheless, a comprehensive understanding of how AEFs influence adsorption properties of molecular species on TM remains incomplete. The adsorption of specific molecules on Pd surfaces under an AEF has been scarcely studied [45]. Yang et al. conducted a detailed investigation of methanol adsorption on Pd(111) under AEF. They found that in the absence of an electric field, methanol adsorbs through either the methyl or hydroxyl group with similar adsorption energies on Pd(111).

However, as AEF intensity increases, the methyl configuration undergoes a slight change, with the angle between the C–O bond and Pd(111) decreasing to 1.9◦, while the hydroxyl configuration remains unaffected. The influence of AEFs on catalytic reactions has been explored in several studies. Che et al. demonstrated that on Ni-based surface in the presence of a positive field, CHx species are more likely to be oxidized by surface O atoms, whereas a negative field enhances oxidation by surface hydroxyl groups of CHx [46]. Earlier, the same group found that a negative electric field accelerates the water dehydrogenation reaction on Ni [42]. Both studies [42,46] employed AEF ranging from -1 V/Å to 1 V/Å. Yeh et al. investigated CH4 partial oxidation with O2on IrO2 surfaces, and revealed that electric fields ranging from -0.6 to 0.6 V/Å have minimal impact on methane dehydrogenation and C–O coupling reactions but significantly affect adsorption processes [47]. DFT combined with microkinetic modeling has been used to study the influence of AEFs on reaction rates and equilibrium constants [39,46]. Che et al. have studied the SMR reaction on Ni-based systems under AEF and showed that electric fields alter the rate and equilibrium constant of the reaction, leading to a slight change of SMR mechanism [39].

Furthermore, they predicted a 27.6 % conversion in the absence of an electric field, which increased to 47.9 % under the application of a positive electric field at 1073 K and 1 bar. Although the experimental results showed lower conversions of 9.8 % without an electric field and 29.5 % with a positive electric field at the same temperature and pressure, the trends were consistent [39]. Moreover, catalytic steam reforming of methane at a low temperature of 423 K showed zero conversion in the absence of the electric field, while in the presence of the electric field, 40 % conversion was obtained [37]. Research on the impact of AEFs on hydrogen transport in Pd membranes is notably absent. The only relevant work is by Rivera and Muhich, who recently demonstrated that a strong negative electric field (below -1.5 V/Å) suppresses the adsorption of H2S, without affecting H2 adsorption on Pd membranes, potentially enhancing the hydrogen flux [43]. Hence, the impact of AEF on hydrogen transport in Pd-based membrane separators and reactors remains unexplored. In this work, we apply multi-scale modeling to assess the intensification of Pd membrane separators and reactors by AEF. The paper is divided into 3 parts. Each part introduces the relevant computational details first, before presenting the results and conclusions. First, we examine the influence of electric fields ranging from -1 to +1 V/Å on the adsorption energies of CO and H2 using DFT calculations. Next, we investigate how the impact of an electric field on co-adsorption of CO leads to intensification in the separation performance of a simple membrane separation unit. In the last section, we study a membrane reactor system conducting methane steam reforming for pure hydrogen production. In this case, we also consider the impact AEF has on the equilibrium constant of the MSR and WGS reactions. 2. The impact of AEF on adsorption energy 2.1. Computational details In order to determine the adsorption energy of CO and H2 on the surface of Pd(111), as well as the activation energy barriers for the diffusion of H atoms within the Pd layers, both in the absence and in the presence of an external electric field, we performed total energy calculations using density functional theory (DFT).

content: , both in the absence and in the presence of an external electric field, we performed total energy calculations using density functional theory (DFT). Our DFT calculations are based on the PBE exchange–correlation functional within the Generalized Gradient Approximation [48] and projector augmented wave (PAW) method [49,50] implemented in the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP) [51,52]. We used Monkhorst–Pack k-point mesh of 10 * 10 * 10 for bulk calculations, 4 * 4 * 1 for slab calculations and Gamma-points for molecules in gas-phase to sample the Brillouin zone and 500 eV as the cutoff energy for the plane wave basis set, which is sufficient for accurate DFT calculations. For slab calculations, we applied a dipole correction to obtain the correct values of the total energies of the slab. For atomic relaxations into their ground state structures, we applied the conjugate-gradient algorithm. We considered that the geometries achieve their equilibrium positions once the atomic forces were smaller than 0.010 eV/Å on each atom, and a total energy convergence was 10-6 eV. Pd bulk and surface. The initial geometry of bulk Pd with fcc structure were taken from Materials Project [53]. For the optimization of the bulk structure we applied a stress tensor and forces, allowing cell shape and volume to change. Our calculated equilibrium lattice parameter 𝑎 for Pd fcc bulk is 3.94 Å, which is in a good agreement with experimental results [54] and DFT findings [55].

Fig. 2 presents PDOS for clean and molecule-adsorbed Pd(111). For the clean Pd(111), PDOS is shown separately for surface and bulk Pd atoms. In the case of molecule-adsorbed Pd(111), we distinguish between unbound surface Pd atoms and those directly interacting with the adsorbates. The PDOS of the clean Pd(111) surface is dominated by Pd 4d states, which span from approximately -7 eV up to the Fermi level (set at 0 eV and indicated by a vertical dashed line). The calculated d-band center (𝑑𝑏) for surface atoms is -1.62 eV, which is 0.36 eV higher than that of bulk Pd (-1.98 eV), reflecting the increased reactivity of the under-coordinated surface atoms. Upon molecular adsorption, the interaction between the adsorbates and the Pd surface leads to a shift in the 𝑑-states of the Pd atoms directly involved in bonding. For instance, Pd atoms bonded to CO and H (green line) exhibit a slight shift in 𝑑𝑏 compared to clean surface Pd atoms (black line), with values of -1.25 eV and -1.50 eV, respectively. These shifts, particularly near the Fermi level, indicate electronic perturbations due to molecule–metal interactions. In the case of H2 adsorption, the shift is more pronounced: the 𝑑𝑏 of the Pd atom bound to H2 is -2.28 eV, compared to -1.62 eV for other surface Pd atoms.

content: The adsorption energies for CO, H2 and dissociated 2H on Pd(111) with respect to the intensity of the applied electric field are presented in Fig. 5. We observed different trends in the adsorption energy for CO, H2 and dissociated 2H on the Pd(111) surface. The adsorption energy increases (in absolute values) from -1.73 eV to -2.15 eV for CO and reduces from -0.32 eV to -0.14 eV for H2 in the range of -1 to +1 V/Å. These values correspond to a change from +0.26 to -0.16 eV in the adsorption energy of CO when compared to its values in the absence of an electric field, while this range is much smaller for H2, which is from -0.07 to +0.11 eV, and for dissociated 2H, it corresponds to the values of 0.00–0.04 eV. This suggests that the electric field has a greater influence on the adsorption energy of CO molecule, primarily because of its polar nature. H2, being a non-polar molecule, experiences a smaller effect. The least pronounced effect is observed in the case of dissociated 2H, which is the dominant adsorption mechanism of hydrogen. Therefore, applying an electric field of -1 V/Å, results in a reduction of the CO adsorption energy by 0.26 eV when compared to its value in the absence of an electric field. On the other hand, applying an electric field of -1 V/Å increases the adsorption energy of H2 by 0.07 eV, which can enhance the likelihood of H2 adsorption, while the adsorption of H is only minimally affected, changing by 0.04 eV.

Our results are consistent with existing literature regarding the limited impact of electric fields on non-polarized molecules [32,43]. For instance, 𝑅𝑖𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑎 et al. [43] demonstrated that electric fields have a minimal effect on the adsorption of H2 onto the Pd membrane. The influence of AEF on the adsorption properties of CO, H, and H2molecules on the Pd(111) surface was also analyzed through changes in the 𝑑𝑏 of Pd atoms. We found that applying an AEF of -1 V/Å and +1 V/Å resulted in only minor shifts in the 𝑑𝑏 for both clean and molecule-adsorbed Pd surfaces, ranging from 0.01 eV to 0.11 eV, suggesting that the AEF does not significantly alter the electronic structure of the systems. Fig. 6 illustrates the charge density difference and Bader charge for the lowest energy configurations of CO, H, and H2 adsorbed on Pd(111), highlighting only the topmost Pd layer. The key findings can be summarized as follows. There is a charge redistribution between Pd layers in the Pd slab in the absence of electric field: negative charge accumulates at the surface and bottom layers (total charge of -0.27𝑒 and -0.28𝑒, respectively), positive charges are at the submonolayers (+0.24𝑒and +0.25𝑒, respectively) and almost zero charge (+0.06𝑒) at the middle layer.

Applying electric field results in the charge repartition on the surface and bottom Pd(111) layers that are extended to the vacuum, such as less negative charge of -0.02𝑒 at 𝐸ext of -1 V/Å and more negative charge of -0.52𝑒 at 𝐸ext of +1 V/Å. The bottom layers are charged accordingly, i.e. more negative (-0.52𝑒) at 𝐸ext of -1 V/Å and less negative charge (-0.01𝑒) at 𝐸ext of +1 V/Å. When molecules are adsorbed on Pd(111) in the absence of electric field, a negative charge accumulates on the CO molecule (-0.22𝑒) due to the higher electronegativity of C (2.55) and O (3.44) atoms compared to Pd (2.20). In contrast, there is an almost negligible charge on H/H2 -0.09𝑒/+0.05𝑒 due to the similar electronegativity of H and Pd. At that charges on the topmost and bottom layers of Pd(111) are also influenced. Applying electric field on molecule/Pd(111) system results in less negative charge on molecule at 𝐸ext of -1 V/Å and more negative charge at 𝐸ext of +1 V/Å, e.g. -0.15𝑒 and -0.34𝑒 for CO on Pd(111), respectively. Thus, the amount of charge on the molecule directly correlates with the variation in the adsorption energy both in the absence and in the presence of an electric field, i.e. a higher charge on CO corresponds to a higher change in the adsorption energy, e.g. CO shows a higher variation in charge and 𝐸ad0 compared to H2.

We found that the activation energy barriers for the diffusion of hydrogen atoms within Pd layers are not affected by electric fields. We observed the variations of the activation energy barriers for the diffusion of hydrogen atoms into Pd layers in a narrow range from 0.001 to 0.03 eV compared to the activation energy barriers calculated without electric fields. Membrane separation of H2 from mixtures containing CO The transmembrane hydrogen flux, and thus the separation performance per membrane area, is affected by the adsorption of other species on the Pd surface, which decreases the surface area available for hydrogen [25,27,28,30,71]. content: We demonstrated in the previous section that an electric field can affect the surface coverage significantly. To gain a better understanding of the electric field’s impact on a separator, we used the estimated binding energy to solve the model Eqs. (17)–(20) under conditions of 550 ◦C, 30 bar, feed of H2/N2/CO=30/69/1 %mol and Pe-1𝑚= 10. Fig. 10(a) illustrates the average surface coverage at electric field strengths ranging from -1 to 1 V/Å. Based on the DFT calculations, a negative electric field leads to a higher surface coverage of hydrogen and a reduced surface coverage of CO. The reduction in surface coverage of CO is significant, dropping from 𝜃CO = 0.89 in the absence of an electric field to 𝜃CO = 0.32 under a negative electric field of -1 V/Å, a 64 % reduction. As shown in Fig. 10(b), the highest hydrogen flux and recovery rates are observed at the most negative electric field, where the CO surface coverage is the lowest. This increases the flux by 2.5 times compared to normal operation. On the other hand, the flux and recovery rates decrease under a positive field due to the higher CO surface coverage. A more comprehensive analysis, including an examination of the effect of the Pe𝑚 number and temperature on the system’s performance (hydrogen recovery), is presented in Fig. 11 at three different temperatures: 450 ◦C, 550 ◦C and 650 ◦C. Higher temperature leads to better recovery.

The way we have defined the Péclet number enables to analyze the performance of an isothermal membrane reactor without considering the temperature’s impact on permeance, which is temperature-dependent. Therefore, the main effect of temperature in the different cases is the surface coverage of competitive species, in this case CO. From the adsorption isotherm Fig. 7, it can be seen that higher temperatures result in lower CO surface coverage, with significant surface coverage of CO at 450 ◦C leading to negligible permeance and recovery. In all the three cases, a larger Pe-1𝑚 leads to higher recovery due to increased surface area for hydrogen transfer.

content: 4. The impact of AEF on a membrane reactor performance In this section, we address a membrane reactor for the intensification of the methane steam reforming reaction, as an example for a key process utilizing Pd membranes. In the case of a packed bed that utilizes a porous catalyst, the orientation of the electric field differs depending on the specific orientation of the catalyst particle. As a result, the AEF’s impact on the reaction kinetics may be largely offset [32]. The method of applying the electric field in a membrane reactor is crucial; whether throughout the entire reactor and affecting the reaction or only near the membrane surface, will impact reactor performance in different ways. As proposed, applying a current through a catalytic surface can create a high electric field [42,44,79]. This method may also be used to affect the metal membrane mainly while generating less influence on the catalyst and the reaction. In order to avoid the complexity of considering actual kinetics we assume that the reaction is at local equilibrium, which is a good assumption in the common case that the transmembrane flow is the limiting rate in the membrane reactor [80–82]. Therefore, we have analyzed two different models to account for the electric field’s impact on a membrane reactor. In the first model, we assume the reaction equilibrium, Eqs. (29) and (30), is unaffected by the field, and is based on parameters listed in Table 2.

In the second model, we consider the change in the thermodynamic equilibrium which may be caused by the electric field [33], by estimating the reactions 𝛥G using DFT calculations [46]. This may impact performance in locations where both the AEF and catalysts are present. 4.1. Formulation of the membrane reactor model In this section we also consider the adsorption of other molecules involved in the methane steam reforming reaction, namely CH4, CO2, and H2O, using published values for their adsorption. We neglect the effect of the electric field on these species, as their adsorption is considered weaker [30,71]. In this case there are five unknowns, the reaction coordinate of both MSR (𝑥1) and WGS (𝑥2), the coverage of H∗ (𝜃H) and CO∗ (𝜃CO), and the hydrogen molar flow rate FH2. We defined the system using dimensionless variables that represent flow rates, reaction coordinates, and length. By using a membrane in the reactor, hydrogen is removed from the system, causing the reaction to shift towards the products. Therefore, a higher hydrogen flux through the membrane should result in a greater conversion. When a negative electric field is applied, the model predicts an increase in conversion from 35 % up to 70 %. This improvement is due to the lower concentration of CO on the surface, which enhances the hydrogen flux, leading to a shift in the reaction towards the products. In a positive electric field, the high surface coverage of CO reduces the hydrogen flux, thereby decreasing the conversion to the equilibrium level. The effects of temperatures and Péclet on the reactor performance can be observed in Figs. 13 and 14. As per the separator model, the Péclet number is used to analyze the membrane-reactor’s performance. Given that the SMR reaction is endothermic, higher temperatures lead to increased conversion. Higher Péclet allows for higher flux due to the larger membrane surface area when comparing the same temperatures, leading to improved conversion and recovery. Applying a negative electric field enhances system performance compared to a case without an electric field. This improvement allows for a smaller membrane area to be used.

Conclusions In this study, we employed a multi-scale modeling approach to investigate the influence of an applied electric field (AEF) on Pdbased membrane separators and reactors. At the molecular scale, DFT calculations were used to estimate key properties, including the adsorption energies of CO on Pd surfaces and the Gibbs free energies of the steam–methane reforming and water–gas shift reactions. These insights were integrated into unit-scale simulations through dimensionless component balances to model the behavior of Pd-membrane systems. Our DFT results revealed that the adsorption energy of polar CO molecules is significantly affected by the presence of an electric field, whereas nonpolar H2 remains almost unaffected. Specifically, a negative electric field of -1 V/Å relative to the Pd surface reduces the CO adsorption energy by 0.26 eV, while a positive field increases it, thereby hindering performance. Additionally, hydrogen diffusion within the Pd lattice was found to be insensitive to the electric field. These molecular-scale findings were incorporated into a onedimensional model of a membrane separator and reactor under an applied electric field.

citation.title: Electric field-driven intensification of palladium membrane separators and reactors: A multi-scale computational study

content: eld. These molecular-scale findings were incorporated into a onedimensional model of a membrane separator and reactor under an applied electric field. Under operating conditions of 550 ◦C, 30 bar, and a feed composition of H2/N2/CO = 30/69/1 %mol, with a dimensionless permeation parameter Pe-1𝑚= 10, the application of a -1 V/Å electric field reduced CO surface coverage by 64 % and enhanced hydrogen flux by a factor of 2.5. This improvement translates to more efficient utilization of the costly Pd membranes. In modeling the membrane reactor, we considered two key effects of the AEF: Competitive adsorption and its influence on reaction equilibrium constants. When only competitive adsorption is considered, the model predicts a substantial increase in conversion at 550 ◦C - from 35 % to 70 %. However, when the AEF’s impact on reaction Gibbs free energy is included, DFT results indicate a detrimental effect on SMR equilibrium conversion. This finding underscores that while AEFs can enhance membrane performance, they may simultaneously impair reactor efficiency if they alter reaction equilibria unfavorably. Therefore, we recommend exploring reactor designs that localize the electric field near the membrane surface, minimizing its influence on bulk reaction equilibria while maximizing its benefits for selective adsorption and hydrogen permeation.
